---
title: "B.1.1 The Three-Way GCC-Moderate Compliance Conflict (TIC 3.0 × Zero Trust × FedRAMP 20x)"
subtitle: "Why GCC-Moderate cannot simultaneously satisfy CASB inspection, continuous device-posture verification, and clean-boundary continuous monitoring — and what the substrate surfaces about it"
doc-type: leaf
audience: [Executive, Customer, Architect, Assessor]
classification: Controlled
boundary: GCC-Moderate
author: Michael Stratton
date: "May 2026"
status: Draft
canon-source: "inbox/New_FedRAMP_Boundary/M365_GCC-Moderate_Telemetry_and_Boundary_Assessment_External_with_images.docx"
canon-reference: "src/uiao/canon/compliance/reference/gcc-moderate-boundary-assessment/"
gap-matrix-data: "src/uiao/canon/data/gcc-moderate-telemetry-gaps.yaml"
parent-leaf: "docs/customer-documents/compliance/boundary-authorization/B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd"
related-narratives:
  - "docs/customer-documents/whitepapers/federal-ssot-alignment.qmd"
  - "docs/customer-documents/orgpath-narrative/07a-uiao-beneath-the-azure-ssot-stack.qmd"
  - "docs/docs/14_TIC3_F5RetirementRoadmap.qmd"
repo-path: docs/customer-documents/compliance/boundary-authorization/B1-1-gcc-moderate-three-way-conflict.qmd
pillar: compliance
sub-category: B
leaf: B.1.1
created-at: 2026-05-11
updated-at: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
  docx:
    toc: true
---

**CONTROLLED**

# B.1.1 The Three-Way GCC-Moderate Compliance Conflict

## 1. Position

The GCC-Moderate boundary model documented in
[B.1](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd) establishes the structural
telemetry constraints, the four capability-disposition classes, and the
ZTMM maturity ceiling that the boundary imposes. This leaf treats the
single most acute compliance finding the boundary produces: a structural
three-way conflict between TIC 3.0, the CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model,
and FedRAMP 20x continuous monitoring requirements, all of which are
mandated for federal civilian agencies and none of which GCC-Moderate
alone can satisfy simultaneously.

The conflict is not a Microsoft product defect. It is a direct consequence
of M365 GCC-Moderate running on Azure Commercial infrastructure: telemetry
and diagnostic data flow to Azure Commercial endpoints, Entra ID tenant
control planes are shared with non-GovCloud tenants, and the location
services TIC 3.0 CASB enforcement assumes are not present at the
GCC-Moderate boundary. Each of the three federal mandates was designed
against a clean cloud boundary assumption that GCC-Moderate, by
architecture, does not provide.

Agencies migrating into a GCC-Moderate tenant structurally inherit this
conflict the moment Entra ID becomes their authoritative identity plane.
The conflict does not surface during the migration itself — it surfaces
during the next ATO renewal, the next 3PAO assessment, or the next CISA
inspection of Zero Trust maturity. The substrate's role is to make the
conflict an explicit, attributed, continuously verified finding before
that point — not after.

## 2. The Three-Way Conflict

The three mandates form a structural triangle in which each pair has a
specific conflict against the GCC-Moderate architecture. None of the three
can be dropped — all three are mandated for federal civilian agencies —
and no single compensating control resolves all three simultaneously.

| Mandate axis | Requires | GCC-Moderate provides | Structural conflict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TIC 3.0** | CASB inspection of all cloud service traffic with policy decisions referencing source/destination identity, geolocation, and device posture | Telemetry routes to Azure Commercial multi-tenant analytics endpoints that bypass the agency's TIC inspection point; geolocation signal fidelity reduced; CASB enforcement at the boundary cannot inspect what the boundary forwards to commercial endpoints | The inspection point exists, but the data flow that would feed it is structurally routed around it |
| **CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model** | Continuous verified device posture as input to every access decision; real-time identity risk signals from ML-driven analytics | Hybrid-joined devices in GCC-Moderate tenants have degraded posture-signal fidelity (Adoption Score unavailable; Endpoint Analytics Advanced and Intune behavioral signals reduced — see [B.1 §3](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd)); real-time Identity Protection ML risk gated by the same boundary | Initial-tier Identity and Devices pillars without significant agency analytics — see [B.1 §4](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd) |
| **FedRAMP 20x continuous monitoring** | Continuous evidence of control effectiveness across a coherent authorization boundary; rapid-detection assumptions assume the boundary is observable | The boundary is observable for control logging but not for the higher-order behavioral analytics that "rapid detection and investigation" requires (CISA BOD 25-01 intent — see [B.1 §5](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd)); FedRAMP 20x's Minimum Assessment Scope refinement is the future path, not the current state | Core logging meets the letter; observability for rapid detection requires agency-built analytics that themselves require export the boundary constrains |

The triangle is structural because each axis depends on telemetry the
boundary either constrains, redirects, or degrades, and the three axes
make non-equivalent demands on that same telemetry. Closing one corner
does not close the other two.

### 2.1 TIC 3.0 — Inspection Point Without Inspectable Flow

TIC 3.0 explicitly models cloud service consumption as a flow that the
agency's Trusted Internet Connection provider must inspect, classify, and
make policy decisions against. The architecture assumes the flow is
observable at the TIC point. In GCC-Moderate, a meaningful fraction of the
telemetry the agency would want to inspect — diagnostic data, audit
signal, Entra ID risk-evaluation traffic — does not transit the TIC
boundary in inspectable form because it routes from the Microsoft tenant
control plane to Microsoft's commercial multi-tenant analytics pipelines
through Microsoft-controlled paths that are not the agency's TIC.

The TIC 3.0 CASB use-case library describes the policy actions an agency
should be able to take on inspectable cloud traffic. Many of those actions
remain available against the traffic GCC-Moderate does route through the
agency network. The conflict is that the policy decisions an agency wants
to make on the basis of identity risk, behavioral anomaly, or
machine-learning-derived classification depend on signals produced by the
commercial pipelines the boundary structurally bypasses. The agency can
inspect what flows through the TIC. The agency cannot make TIC 3.0
policy decisions of the fidelity TIC 3.0 envisions against signals the
boundary keeps from reaching the TIC.

### 2.2 CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model — Posture Verification at Initial Tier

The CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model v2.0 requires that device posture be
continuously verified and that identity risk be evaluated in real time as
inputs to every access decision. The Maturity Model classifies these
capabilities at the Advanced and Optimal tiers when they are continuous,
behavior-aware, and ML-driven; at the Initial tier when they are
periodic, static, and rule-based.

Hybrid-joined devices in a GCC-Moderate tenant are subject to the
degradations the boundary imposes on the underlying telemetry. The
Endpoint Analytics Advanced and Intune behavioral analytics that feed
Advanced-tier posture verification are constrained — see the disposition
table in [B.1 §3](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd).
Real-time Identity Protection ML risk and Continuous Access Evaluation
are similarly gated for the same architectural reason. Per the maturity
table in [B.1 §4](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd),
GCC-Moderate alone caps near Initial in Identity, Devices, and Networks
unless the agency builds its own analytics to compensate.

The conflict is that the Zero Trust Executive Order (EO 14028) and OMB
M-22-09 do not contemplate that the underlying platform might constrain
the signals continuous verification requires. The mandate assumes the
signals are available; the boundary is the reason they are not.

### 2.3 FedRAMP 20x Continuous Monitoring — Clean-Boundary Assumption

FedRAMP 20x continuous monitoring contemplates an authorization boundary
that is both coherent (every artifact lives inside the boundary or is
explicitly scoped out) and observable (the boundary's behavior is
continuously evidenced through the substrate that produces the
authorization package). FedRAMP 20x's Minimum Assessment Scope work — the
MAS-CSO-IIR and MAS-CSO-MDI refinements — is the program's response to
the recognition that some service-provider analytics pipelines should be
removed from the agency's authorization scope rather than blocked at the
network boundary.

This is the right structural answer over time. It is not the current
state. Today, the GCC-Moderate boundary is observable for the controls
FedRAMP Moderate enumerates (the core logging categories of CISA BOD
25-01 and OMB M-21-31) but not for the behavioral and ML-derived analytics
that CISA BOD 25-01's "rapid detection and investigation" intent requires
— see [B.1 §5](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd).
The conflict is that FedRAMP 20x's continuous-monitoring narrative assumes
behavioral observability that the boundary, in its current scope, does
not provide.

## 3. Purview Information Protection Telemetry Constraint

The three-way conflict described above is the headline finding. A specific
data-governance corollary deserves its own treatment because it propagates
into every downstream Azure SSOT trust assertion: Microsoft Purview
Information Protection features — scan-driven sensitivity classification,
adaptive policy evaluation, lineage attribution to Entra ID principals —
depend on telemetry and policy signals that traverse the Azure Commercial
boundary when running under GCC-Moderate.

The consequence is that every Purview governance assertion in a
GCC-Moderate tenant inherits the boundary's constraint on the underlying
identity-state telemetry. A sensitivity label applied by a principal whose
authorization state has drifted is not a compliant control if the drift
itself is not observable through the boundary the label policy depends on.
A lineage record attributing a change to a principal in a state the
boundary's telemetry cannot continuously verify is an evidence artifact
whose strength is bounded by that verification gap.

This is the architectural reason the
[OrgPath Narrative chapter 07a](../../orgpath-narrative/07a-uiao-beneath-the-azure-ssot-stack.qmd)
positions the substrate as the identity governance layer *beneath* the
Microsoft Fabric + OneLake + Purview Azure SSOT stack: the trust
assertions Purview makes about data assets are only as reliable as the
identity-state telemetry the boundary either does or does not deliver.
The substrate's role at the GCC-Moderate boundary is to make the
verification gap that constrains those assertions a continuously
attributed finding, rather than an assumption hidden in the boundary's
fine print.

## 4. UIAO Compensating Controls Surface

The substrate does not remediate the boundary. The boundary is structural,
and Section 6 of [B.1](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd)
describes the MAS 2026 path as the realistic future remediation. What the
substrate does is make the conflict an attributed finding, document the
agency-side compensating controls the conflict requires, and demonstrate
that those controls are continuously verified.

The Compliance Control Plane is the substrate surface that owns these
compensating controls. For each axis of the three-way conflict, the
Compliance Plane records:

- **The mandate the agency is subject to** — TIC 3.0, the Zero Trust
  Maturity Model pillar, or the FedRAMP 20x continuous-monitoring
  requirement.
- **The boundary constraint that conflicts with the mandate** — which
  signal is unavailable, degraded, or routed past the agency's
  inspection point.
- **The compensating control the agency has put in place** — agency-side
  analytics, third-party SASE or UEBA overlay, custom KQL or SIEM rules,
  trust-algorithm overlay in the agency SIEM. The compensating-control
  catalogue in [B.1 §5](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd)
  enumerates the mandate-by-mandate options that are observed in the
  field.
- **The verification surface** — the substrate's own drift findings
  (`DRIFT-PROVENANCE`, `DRIFT-AUTHZ`, `DRIFT-IDENTITY`) that demonstrate
  the compensating control is operating, and the cadence on which the
  Evidence Bundle records the finding.

This is the agency-side counterpart of the boundary-side restriction: the
boundary does what it does, and the Compliance Plane records what the
agency does in response. The pairing is what an assessor consumes to
evaluate whether the compensating posture actually closes the conflict
the boundary creates.

## 5. Evidence Bundle Finding Shape

The substrate emits the three-way conflict as a first-class compliance
finding in the Evidence Bundle. The finding shape is structured rather
than narrative, so an assessor can consume it the same way the substrate's
other artifacts are consumed.

| Field | Content |
|---|---|
| `finding_id` | `GCC-BOUNDARY-3WAY-001` |
| `finding_class` | `compliance-conflict` |
| `severity` | `P1` — structural, agency-wide, persistent until either the boundary or the mandate changes |
| `mandates_affected` | `["TIC-3.0", "CISA-ZTMM", "FedRAMP-20x"]` |
| `boundary` | `gcc-moderate` |
| `gap_matrix_rows` | references into `src/uiao/canon/data/gcc-moderate-telemetry-gaps.yaml` for the specific telemetry rows the conflict depends on |
| `compensating_controls` | array of agency-side controls documented in the Compliance Plane, each with its own verification surface |
| `verification_drift_findings` | references to `DRIFT-PROVENANCE`, `DRIFT-AUTHZ`, `DRIFT-IDENTITY` findings that continuously evidence the compensating controls are operating |
| `remediation_path` | `MAS-2026` (boundary scope refinement) — see [B.1 §6](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd) |
| `evidence_emission_cadence` | per the standard substrate cadence; the finding accumulates rather than being assembled at assessment time |

The finding is intentionally a single artifact rather than three separate
findings, one per mandate, because the conflict is structural across all
three. Splitting it would lose the architectural shape and produce three
narrower findings that an assessor would have to recompose into the
triangle the boundary actually creates. The compensating-controls field
is an array because the agency response is typically a portfolio of
controls — agency analytics + third-party overlay + custom rules — rather
than a single compensating action.

## 6. What This Finding Is and Is Not

This finding is not a vulnerability. It is not a misconfiguration. It is
not something a vendor patch will close. It is a structural compliance
conflict the agency inherits the moment a Microsoft Entra ID GCC-Moderate
tenant becomes its authoritative identity plane, and it remains active
until either the boundary is restructured (the MAS 2026 path) or the
mandates that conflict with the boundary are themselves restructured.

The finding is also not an indictment of Microsoft. Microsoft has been
explicit about what GCC-Moderate provides and where the boundary sits;
the analytical pipelines on the other side of the boundary are real
services that Microsoft is correctly not bringing into FedRAMP-Moderate
scope. The conflict is between the agency-side mandates and the
boundary-side scope decisions. The substrate's role is to make that
relationship explicit, attributed, and continuously verified, so that
the agency's compliance posture under the conflict is itself observable.

## 7. Cross-References

- **Parent boundary model**: [B.1 GCC-Moderate Boundary Model](B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd)
- **Configuration-variant sibling**: [B.1.2 Teams Phone Under TIC 2.0 Centralized DIA, F5 Removed, No SD-WAN](B1-2-teams-phone-tic2-centralized-dia.qmd)
- **Federal mandate alignment whitepaper**: [Federal SSOT Alignment](../../whitepapers/federal-ssot-alignment.qmd)
- **Azure SSOT stack identity-substrate role**: [OrgPath Narrative 07a — UIAO Beneath the Azure SSOT Stack](../../orgpath-narrative/07a-uiao-beneath-the-azure-ssot-stack.qmd)
- **TIC 3.0 F5 retirement roadmap**: [Doc 14](../../../docs/14_TIC3_F5RetirementRoadmap.qmd)
- **Drift taxonomy**: [Doc 16](../../../docs/16_DriftDetectionStandard.qmd)
- **Machine-readable gap matrix**: [`canon/data/gcc-moderate-telemetry-gaps.yaml`]({{< meta gap-matrix-data >}})
- **Source memo**: [`{{< meta canon-source >}}`]({{< meta canon-source >}})

## 8. Provenance

This leaf formalizes the three-way conflict treatment promised by the
Federal SSOT Alignment whitepaper §4 and the OrgPath Narrative chapter
07a §Chapter 7. Its content derives from the M365 GCC-Moderate Telemetry
& Boundary Assessment (External v1.0, 2026-04-25) — the same canon source
B.1 derives from — restructured around the three-axis compliance
triangle rather than the telemetry-capability disposition framing B.1
uses. The yaml gap matrix at
`src/uiao/canon/data/gcc-moderate-telemetry-gaps.yaml` remains the
machine-readable authority for per-capability evidence; this leaf is the
narrative finding artifact built on top of that matrix.
